dailyJudgementSnippet: Anthropic edges the day: HN gave Claude Code real credit for a difficult server migration [E1], while also spending substantial time criticizing Opus 4.7’s limit burn and Claude Design’s practicality [E2][E6].
winnerExplanation: Anthropic had the most substantial coverage and the only clearly strong positive anecdote of the day, even though much of the rest of the discussion was critical. OpenAI’s mentions skewed negative on cost and quality comparisons, Microsoft Copilot’s lone relevant thread was a price/performance complaint, and Google Gemini had no meaningful signal.

openai.judgementSnippet: Negative. HN comments discussed GPT-5/o3 mostly as expensive and poor on cost-effectiveness, with smaller comparison chatter also framing GPT/Codex less favorably than Claude.

anthropic.judgementSnippet: Mixed but strongest overall presence. Claude Code got notable praise for handling a messy migration, but Opus 4.7 and Claude Design drew recurring complaints about fast quota burn, opaque pricing/limits, and limited practical value.

microsoft_copilot.judgementSnippet: Negative. Relevant discussion focused on VSCode Copilot replacing preferred Opus options with a higher usage modifier, seen as a price/performance regression.

evidence.summary: On the Hetzner migration story, a top commenter said Claude Code migrated two tangled servers with many sites, outdated libraries, MySQL, and Redis, calling complex migrations much easier now; another commenter criticized the Claude-like prose but still acknowledged the savings.

In the Opus 4.6/4.7 discussion, several commenters said 4.7’s improvement was barely noticeable but its quota burn was very noticeable, with complaints about opaque effort settings, faster limit consumption, and benchmark-vs-reality mismatch.

The same Opus thread included direct GPT/Codex comparisons: one user said they would use Codex anyway, but others said Claude was more fun than GPT/Codex and that high-powered GPTs shared the same overthinking flaws.
A commenter using VSCode Copilot said Opus 4.7 would replace 4.5 and 4.6 with a 7.5x modifier, describing the change as a likely price hike for slower performance and a workflow regression.

In the AI agents cost thread, commenters called GPT-5 and o3 expensive frontier options, citing very high hourly costs and poor economics, especially when failure rates are considered or compared with human labor.

The Claude Design discussion skewed skeptical: one user said a single design session used 95% of their weekly quota and called it 'not a real tool,' while other comments argued AI-generated simplicity does not solve the complexity of real products.
